S-THE-040.prose:
Most of those reports were a nightmare—grotesque, circumstantial, eager, and untrue. When Michaelis’s testimony at the inquest brought to light Wilson’s suspicions of his wife I thought the whole tale would shortly be served up in racy pasquinade—but Catherine, who might have said anything, didn’t say a word. She showed a surprising amount of character about it too—looked at the coroner with determined eyes under that corrected brow of hers, and swore that her sister had never seen Gatsby, that her sister was completely happy with her husband, that her sister had been into no mischief whatever. She convinced herself of it, and cried into her handkerchief, as if the very suggestion was more than she could endure. So Wilson was reduced to a man “deranged by grief” in order that the case might remain in its simplest form. And it rested there. But all this part of it seemed remote and unessential. I found myself on Gatsby’s side, and alone. From the moment I telephoned news of the catastrophe to West Egg village, every surmise about him, and every practical question, was referred to me. At first I was surprised and confused; then, as he lay in his house and didn’t move or breathe or speak, hour upon hour, it grew upon me that I was responsible, because no one else was interested—interested, I mean, with that intense personal interest to which everyone has some vague right at the end. I called up Daisy half an hour after we found him, called her instinctively and without hesitation. But she and Tom had gone away early that afternoon, and taken baggage with them. “Left no address?” “No.” “Say when they’d be back?” “No.” “Any idea where they are? How I could reach them?” “I don’t know. Can’t say.” I wanted to get somebody for him. I wanted to go into the room where he lay and reassure him: “I’ll get somebody for you, Gatsby.

Don’t worry. Just trust me and I’ll get somebody for you—” Meyer Wolfshiem’s name wasn’t in the phone book. The butler gave me his office address on Broadway, and I called Information, but by the time I had the number it was long after five, and no one answered the phone. “Will you ring again?” “I’ve rung three times.” “It’s very important.” “Sorry. I’m afraid no one’s there.” I went back to the drawing-room and thought for an instant that they were chance visitors, all these official people who suddenly filled it. But, though they drew back the sheet and looked at Gatsby with shocked eyes, his protest continued in my brain: “Look here, old sport, you’ve got to get somebody for me. You’ve got to try hard. I can’t go through this alone.” Someone started to ask me questions, but I broke away and going upstairs looked hastily through the unlocked parts of his desk—he’d never told me definitely that his parents were dead. But there was nothing—only the picture of Dan Cody, a token of forgotten violence, staring down from the wall. Next morning I sent the butler to New York with a letter to Wolfshiem, which asked for information and urged him to come out on the next train. That request seemed superfluous when I wrote it. I was sure he’d start when he saw the newspapers, just as I was sure there’d be a wire from Daisy before noon—but neither a wire nor Mr. Wolfshiem arrived; no one arrived except more police and photographers and newspaper men. When the butler brought back Wolfshiem’s answer I began to have a feeling of defiance, of scornful solidarity between Gatsby and me against them all. Dear Mr. Carraway. This has been one of the most terrible shocks of my life to me I hardly can believe it that it is true at all.

Such a mad act as that man did should make us all think. I cannot come down now as I am tied up in some very important business and cannot get mixed up in this thing now. If there is anything I can do a little later let me know in a letter by Edgar. I hardly know where I am when I hear about a thing like this and am completely knocked down and out. Yours truly Meyer Wolfshiem and then hasty addenda beneath: Let me know about the funeral etc do not know his family at all. When the phone rang that afternoon and Long Distance said Chicago was calling I thought this would be Daisy at last. But the connection came through as a man’s voice, very thin and far away. “This is Slagle speaking …” “Yes?” The name was unfamiliar. “Hell of a note, isn’t it? Get my wire?” “There haven’t been any wires.” “Young Parke’s in trouble,” he said rapidly. “They picked him up when he handed the bonds over the counter. They got a circular from New York giving ’em the numbers just five minutes before. What d’you know about that, hey? You never can tell in these hick towns—” “Hello!” I interrupted breathlessly. “Look here—this isn’t Mr. Gatsby. Mr. Gatsby’s dead.

” There was a long silence on the other end of the wire, followed by an exclamation … then a quick squawk as the connection was broken. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ I think it was on the third day that a telegram signed Henry C. Gatz arrived from a town in Minnesota. It said only that the sender was leaving immediately and to postpone the funeral until he came. It was Gatsby’s father, a solemn old man, very helpless and dismayed, bundled up in a long cheap ulster against the warm September day.
S-THE-040.summary: In the chaotic aftermath, Nick finds himself alone in his grief for Gatsby. Catherine, Myrtle's sister, falsely testifies at the inquest to protect Myrtle's reputation. Nick desperately tries to reach Daisy and Meyer Wolfshiem, but both prove elusive or unwilling to help. Daisy and Tom flee without a trace, and Wolfshiem sends a cold, evasive letter. A phone call from Slagle reveals Gatsby's continued involvement in bond fraud, further tarnishing his image. Finally, Gatsby's estranged father, Henry C. Gatz, arrives from Minnesota, a solemn figure amidst the abandonment.

S-THE-041.prose:
incessantly at his sparse grey beard that I had difficulty in getting off his coat. He was on the point of collapse, so I took him into the music-room and made him sit down while I sent for something to eat. But he wouldn’t eat, and the glass of milk spilled from his trembling hand. “I saw it in the Chicago newspaper,” he said. “It was all in the Chicago newspaper. I started right away.” “I didn’t know how to reach you.” His eyes, seeing nothing, moved ceaselessly about the room. “It was a madman,” he said. “He must have been mad.” “Wouldn’t you like some coffee?” I urged him. “I don’t want anything. I’m all right now, Mr.—” “Carraway.” “Well, I’m all right now. Where have they got Jimmy?” I took him into the drawing-room, where his son lay, and left him there. Some little boys had come up on the steps and were looking into the hall; when I told them who had arrived, they went reluctantly away. After a little while Mr. Gatz opened the door and came out, his mouth ajar, his face flushed slightly, his eyes leaking isolated and unpunctual tears. He had reached an age where death no longer has the quality of ghastly surprise, and when he looked around him now for the first time and saw the height and splendour of the hall and the great rooms opening out from it into other rooms, his grief began to be mixed with an awed pride. I helped him to a bedroom upstairs; while he took off his coat and vest I told him that all arrangements had been deferred until he came. “I didn’t know what you’d want, Mr. Gatsby—” “Gatz is my name.

” “—Mr. Gatz. I thought you might want to take the body West.” He shook his head. “Jimmy always liked it better down East. He rose up to his position in the East. Were you a friend of my boy’s, Mr.—?” “We were close friends.” “He had a big future before him, you know. He was only a young man, but he had a lot of brain power here.” He touched his head impressively, and I nodded. “If he’d of lived, he’d of been a great man. A man like James J. Hill. He’d of helped build up the country.” “That’s true,” I said, uncomfortably. He fumbled at the embroidered coverlet, trying to take it from the bed, and lay down stiffly—was instantly asleep. That night an obviously frightened person called up, and demanded to know who I was before he would give his name. “This is Mr. Carraway,” I said. “Oh!” He sounded relieved. “This is Klipspringer.” I was relieved too, for that seemed to promise another friend at Gatsby’s grave.
S-THE-041.summary: Nick nervously awaits Gatsby’s father, who arrives disoriented and grieving, clinging to the memory of his son's ambition. Nick leads him to Gatsby’s body, and Mr. Gatz, despite his sorrow, expresses immense pride in the grandeur of the house and Gatsby's achievements.

C-THE-01.transition: Traveled to New York to meet Meyer Wolfshiem.

S-THE-042.prose:
I didn’t want it to be in the papers and draw a sightseeing crowd, so I’d been calling up a few people myself. They were hard to find. “The funeral’s tomorrow,” I said. “Three o’clock, here at the house. I wish you’d tell anybody who’d be interested.” “Oh, I will,” he broke out hastily. “Of course I’m not likely to see anybody, but if I do.” His tone made me suspicious. “Of course you’ll be there yourself.” “Well, I’ll certainly try. What I called up about is—” “Wait a minute,” I interrupted. “How about saying you’ll come?” “Well, the fact is—the truth of the matter is that I’m staying with some people up here in Greenwich, and they rather expect me to be with them tomorrow. In fact, there’s a sort of picnic or something. Of course I’ll do my best to get away.” I ejaculated an unrestrained “Huh!” and he must have heard me, for he went on nervously: “What I called up about was a pair of shoes I left there. I wonder if it’d be too much trouble to have the butler send them on. You see, they’re tennis shoes, and I’m sort of helpless without them. My address is care of B. F.—” I didn’t hear the rest of the name, because I hung up the receiver. After that I felt a certain shame for Gatsby—one gentleman to whom I telephoned implied that he had got what he deserved. However, that was my fault, for he was one of those who used to sneer most bitterly at Gatsby on the courage of Gatsby’s liquor, and I should have known better than to call him. The morning of the funeral I went up to New York to see Meyer Wolfshiem; I couldn’t seem to reach him any other way.

The door that I pushed open, on the advice of an elevator boy, was marked “The Swastika Holding Company,” and at first there didn’t seem to be anyone inside. But when I’d shouted “hello” several times in vain, an argument broke out behind a partition, and presently a lovely Jewess appeared at an interior door and scrutinized me with black hostile eyes. “Nobody’s in,” she said. “Mr. Wolfshiem’s gone to Chicago.” The first part of this was obviously untrue, for someone had begun to whistle “The Rosary,” tunelessly, inside. “Please say that Mr. Carraway wants to see him.” “I can’t get him back from Chicago, can I?” At this moment a voice, unmistakably Wolfshiem’s, called “Stella!” from the other side of the door. “Leave your name on the desk,” she said quickly. “I’ll give it to him when he gets back.” “But I know he’s there.” She took a step toward me and began to slide her hands indignantly up and down her hips. “You young men think you can force your way in here any time,” she scolded. “We’re getting sickantired of it. When I say he’s in Chicago, he’s in Chicago.” I mentioned Gatsby. “Oh-h!” She looked at me over again. “Will you just—What was your name?” She vanished. In a moment Meyer Wolfshiem stood solemnly in the doorway, holding out both hands. He drew me into his office, remarking in a reverent voice that it was a sad time for all of us, and offered me a cigar.

“My memory goes back to when first I met him,” he said. “A young major just out of the army and covered over with medals he got in the war. He was so hard up he had to keep on wearing his uniform because he couldn’t buy some regular clothes. First time I saw him was when he came into Winebrenner’s poolroom at Forty-third Street and asked for a job. He hadn’t eat anything for a couple of days. ‘Come on have some lunch with me,’ I said. He ate more than four dollars’ worth of food in half an hour.” “Did you start him in business?” I inquired. “Start him! I made him.” “Oh.” “I raised him up out of nothing, right out of the gutter. I saw right away he was a fine-appearing, gentlemanly young man, and when he told me he was at Oggsford I knew I could use him good. I got him to join the American Legion and he used to stand high there. Right off he did some work for a client of mine up to Albany. We were so thick like that in everything”—he held up two bulbous fingers—“always together.” I wondered if this partnership had included the World’s Series transaction in 1919. “Now he’s dead,” I said after a moment. “You were his closest friend, so I know you’ll want to come to his funeral this afternoon.” “I’d like to come.” “Well, come then.” The hair in his nostrils quivered slightly, and as he shook his head his eyes filled with tears. “I can’t do it—I can’t get mixed up in it,” he said. “There’s nothing to get mixed up in.

It’s all over now.” “When a man gets killed I never like to get mixed up in it in any way. I keep out. When I was a young man it was different—if a friend of mine died, no matter how, I stuck with them to the end. You may think that’s sentimental, but I mean it—to the bitter end.” I saw that for some reason of his own he was determined not to come, so I stood up. “Are you a college man?” he inquired suddenly. For a moment I thought he was going to suggest a “gonnegtion,” but he only nodded and shook my hand. “Let us learn to show our friendship for a man when he is alive and not after he is dead,” he suggested. “After that my own rule is to let everything alone.” When I left his office the sky had turned dark and I got back to West Egg in a drizzle. After changing my clothes I went next door and found Mr. Gatz walking up and down excitedly in the hall. His pride in his son and in his son’s possessions was continually increasing and now he had something to show me. “Jimmy sent me this picture.” He took out his wallet with trembling fingers. “Look there.” It was a photograph of the house, cracked in the corners and dirty with many hands. He pointed out every detail to me eagerly. “Look there!” and then sought admiration from my eyes. He had shown it so often that I think it was more real to him now than the house itself. “Jimmy sent it to me. I think it’s a very pretty picture.
S-THE-042.summary: Nick makes desperate calls to secure attendees for Gatsby's funeral, facing blatant rejections and self-serving excuses from former associates like Klipspringer. His attempt to recruit Meyer Wolfshiem is met with evasion and a cold refusal, as Wolfshiem recounts his role in Gatsby's rise, but distances himself from the deceased's fate.

continuityMutations.content: Mr. Gatz carries a childhood schedule of Gatsby's, revealing his early ambition and self-discipline.
Owl-Eyes is the only 'friend' from Gatsby's parties to attend the funeral, highlighting his genuine character.

Owl-Eyes is the only previous party guest who attends Gatsby's funeral, showing genuine respect.

C-THE-01.transition: Traveled in a limousine with Mr. Gatz and the minister to the cemetery.

C-THE-122.transition: Traveled in a limousine with Nick and the minister to the cemetery.

S-THE-043.prose:
It shows up well.” “Very well. Had you seen him lately?” “He come out to see me two years ago and bought me the house I live in now. Of course we was broke up when he run off from home, but I see now there was a reason for it. He knew he had a big future in front of him. And ever since he made a success he was very generous with me.” He seemed reluctant to put away the picture, held it for another minute, lingeringly, before my eyes. Then he returned the wallet and pulled from his pocket a ragged old copy of a book called Hopalong Cassidy. “Look here, this is a book he had when he was a boy. It just shows you.” He opened it at the back cover and turned it around for me to see. On the last flyleaf was printed the word schedule, and the date September 12, 1906. And underneath: Rise from bed 6:00 a.m. Dumbell exercise and wall-scaling 6:15-6:30 ” Study electricity, etc. 7:15-8:15 ” Work 8:30-4:30 p.m. Baseball and sports 4:30-5:00 ” Practise elocution, poise and how to attain it 5:00-6:00 ” Study needed inventions 7:00-9:00 ” General Resolves * No wasting time at Shafters or [a name, indecipherable] * No more smokeing or chewing. * Bath every other day * Read one improving book or magazine per week * Save $5.00 [crossed out] $3.00 per week * Be better to parents “I came across this book by accident,” said the old man. “It just shows you, don’t it?” “It just shows you.” “Jimmy was bound to get ahead.

He always had some resolves like this or something. Do you notice what he’s got about improving his mind? He was always great for that. He told me I et like a hog once, and I beat him for it.” He was reluctant to close the book, reading each item aloud and then looking eagerly at me. I think he rather expected me to copy down the list for my own use. A little before three the Lutheran minister arrived from Flushing, and I began to look involuntarily out the windows for other cars. So did Gatsby’s father. And as the time passed and the servants came in and stood waiting in the hall, his eyes began to blink anxiously, and he spoke of the rain in a worried, uncertain way. The minister glanced several times at his watch, so I took him aside and asked him to wait for half an hour. But it wasn’t any use. Nobody came. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ About five o’clock our procession of three cars reached the cemetery and stopped in a thick drizzle beside the gate—first a motor hearse, horribly black and wet, then Mr. Gatz and the minister and me in the limousine, and a little later four or five servants and the postman from West Egg, in Gatsby’s station wagon, all wet to the skin. As we started through the gate into the cemetery I heard a car stop and then the sound of someone splashing after us over the soggy ground. I looked around. It was the man with owl-eyed glasses whom I had found marvelling over Gatsby’s books in the library one night three months before. I’d never seen him since then. I don’t know how he knew about the funeral, or even his name. The rain poured down his thick glasses, and he took them off and wiped them to see the protecting canvas unrolled from Gatsby’s grave. I tried to think about Gatsby then for a moment, but he was already too far away, and I could only remember, without resentment, that Daisy hadn’t sent a message or a flower. Dimly I heard someone murmur “Blessed are the dead that the rain falls on,” and then the owl-eyed man said “Amen to that,” in a brave voice. We straggled down quickly through the rain to the cars. Owl-eyes spoke to me by the gate. “I couldn’t get to the house,” he remarked.

“Neither could anybody else.” “Go on!” He started. “Why, my God! they used to go there by the hundreds.” He took off his glasses and wiped them again, outside and in. “The poor son-of-a-bitch,” he said. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ One of my most vivid memories is of coming back West from prep school and later from college at Christmas time. Those who went farther than Chicago would gather in the old dim Union Station at six o’clock of a December evening, with a few Chicago friends, already caught up into their own holiday gaieties, to bid them a hasty goodbye. I remember the fur coats of the girls returning from Miss This-or-That’s and the chatter of frozen breath and the hands waving overhead as we caught sight of old acquaintances, and the matchings of invitations: “Are you going to the Ordways’? the Herseys’? the Schultzes’?” and the long green tickets clasped tight in our gloved hands. And last the murky yellow cars of the Chicago, Milwaukee and St. Paul railroad looking cheerful as Christmas itself on the tracks beside the gate. When we pulled out into the winter night and the real snow, our snow, began to stretch out beside us and twinkle against the windows, and the dim lights of small Wisconsin stations moved by, a sharp wild brace came suddenly into the air. We drew in deep breaths of it as we walked back from dinner through the cold vestibules, unutterably aware of our identity with this country for one strange hour, before we melted indistinguishably into it again. That’s my Middle West—not the wheat or the prairies or the lost Swede towns, but the thrilling returning trains of my youth, and the street lamps and sleigh bells in the frosty dark and the shadows of holly wreaths thrown by lighted windows on the snow. I am part of that, a little solemn with the feel of those long winters, a little complacent from growing up in the Carraway house in a city where dwellings are still called through decades by a family’s name. I see now that this has been a story of the West, after all—Tom and Gatsby, Daisy and Jordan and I, were all Westerners, and perhaps we possessed some deficiency in common which made us subtly unadaptable to Eastern life. Even when the East excited me most, even when I was most keenly aware of its superiority to the bored, sprawling, swollen towns beyond the Ohio, with their interminable inquisitions which spared only the children and the very old—even then it had always for me a quality of distortion. West Egg, especially, still figures in my more fantastic dreams. I see it as a night scene by El Greco: a hundred houses, at once conventional and grotesque, crouching under a sullen, overhanging sky and a lustreless moon. In the foreground four solemn men in dress suits are walking along the sidewalk with a stretcher on which lies a drunken woman in a white evening dress. Her hand, which dangles over the side, sparkles cold with jewels.
S-THE-043.summary: As the funeral procession forms, Nick, Mr. Gatz, and a few others wait in vain for more attendees; only Owl-Eyes, a former party guest, arrives, expressing genuine sorrow. The somber, rain-drenched burial highlights Gatsby's isolation in death, and Nick reflects on Daisy's cold indifference.

continuityMutations.content: Jordan reveals she is engaged to another man.
Jordan believes Nick is a 'bad driver' and not as honest as she thought.
Tom admits he told Wilson that Gatsby owned the yellow car.
Tom believes Gatsby 'had it coming to him' and justifies his actions by claiming Gatsby ran over Myrtle and threw 'dust' in Daisy's eyes.
Nick recognizes Tom and Daisy as 'careless people' who smash things and retreat into their money.
Jordan reveals she is engaged to another man and confronts Nick for 'throwing her over' on the phone.
Jordan accuses Nick of being a bad driver, metaphorically implying his carelessness in relationships, and expresses disappointment in his perceived dishonesty.
Tom confesses to telling Wilson that Gatsby owned the car that killed Myrtle, but justifies his actions by claiming Gatsby 'had it coming to him' and was a 'tough one'.
Tom expresses self-pity, recounting his tears over seeing dog biscuits in his old flat, believing he suffered greatly.
Tom demonstrates a profound lack of empathy and self-awareness, viewing his destructive actions as justified and Gatsby's death as deserved.

C-THE-01.transition: Decides to leave the East and return home.

S-THE-044.prose:
Gravely the men turn in at a house—the wrong house. But no one knows the woman’s name, and no one cares. After Gatsby’s death the East was haunted for me like that, distorted beyond my eyes’ power of correction. So when the blue smoke of brittle leaves was in the air and the wind blew the wet laundry stiff on the line I decided to come back home. There was one thing to be done before I left, an awkward, unpleasant thing that perhaps had better have been let alone. But I wanted to leave things in order and not just trust that obliging and indifferent sea to sweep my refuse away. I saw Jordan Baker and talked over and around what had happened to us together, and what had happened afterward to me, and she lay perfectly still, listening, in a big chair. She was dressed to play golf, and I remember thinking she looked like a good illustration, her chin raised a little jauntily, her hair the colour of an autumn leaf, her face the same brown tint as the fingerless glove on her knee. When I had finished she told me without comment that she was engaged to another man. I doubted that, though there were several she could have married at a nod of her head, but I pretended to be surprised. For just a minute I wondered if I wasn’t making a mistake, then I thought it all over again quickly and got up to say goodbye. “Nevertheless you did throw me over,” said Jordan suddenly. “You threw me over on the telephone. I don’t give a damn about you now, but it was a new experience for me, and I felt a little dizzy for a while.” We shook hands. “Oh, and do you remember”—she added—“a conversation we had once about driving a car?” “Why—not exactly.” “You said a bad driver was only safe until she met another bad driver? Well, I met another bad driver, didn’t I? I mean it was careless of me to make such a wrong guess. I thought you were rather an honest, straightforward person. I thought it was your secret pride.” “I’m thirty,” I said. “I’m five years too old to lie to myself and call it honour.” She didn’t answer.

Angry, and half in love with her, and tremendously sorry, I turned away. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ One afternoon late in October I saw Tom Buchanan. He was walking ahead of me along Fifth Avenue in his alert, aggressive way, his hands out a little from his body as if to fight off interference, his head moving sharply here and there, adapting itself to his restless eyes. Just as I slowed up to avoid overtaking him he stopped and began frowning into the windows of a jewellery store. Suddenly he saw me and walked back, holding out his hand. “What’s the matter, Nick? Do you object to shaking hands with me?” “Yes. You know what I think of you.” “You’re crazy, Nick,” he said quickly. “Crazy as hell. I don’t know what’s the matter with you.” “Tom,” I inquired, “what did you say to Wilson that afternoon?” He stared at me without a word, and I knew I had guessed right about those missing hours. I started to turn away, but he took a step after me and grabbed my arm. “I told him the truth,” he said. “He came to the door while we were getting ready to leave, and when I sent down word that we weren’t in he tried to force his way upstairs. He was crazy enough to kill me if I hadn’t told him who owned the car. His hand was on a revolver in his pocket every minute he was in the house—” He broke off defiantly. “What if I did tell him? That fellow had it coming to him. He threw dust into your eyes just like he did in Daisy’s, but he was a tough one. He ran over Myrtle like you’d run over a dog and never even stopped his car.” There was nothing I could say, except the one unutterable fact that it wasn’t true. “And if you think I didn’t have my share of suffering—look here, when I went to give up that flat and saw that damn box of dog biscuits sitting there on the sideboard, I sat down and cried like a baby.

By God it was awful—” I couldn’t forgive him or like him, but I saw that what he had done was, to him, entirely justified. It was all very careless and confused. They were careless people, Tom and Daisy—they smashed up things and creatures and then retreated back into their money or their vast carelessness, or whatever it was that kept them together, and let other people clean up the mess they had made … I shook hands with him; it seemed silly not to, for I felt suddenly as though I were talking to a child. Then he went into the jewellery store to buy a pearl necklace—or perhaps only a pair of cuff buttons—rid of my provincial squeamishness forever. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ Gatsby’s house was still empty when I left—the grass on his lawn had grown as long as mine. One of the taxi drivers in the village never took a fare past the entrance gate without stopping for a minute and pointing inside; perhaps it was he who drove Daisy and Gatsby over to East Egg the night of the accident, and perhaps he had made a story about it all his own. I didn’t want to hear it and I avoided him when I got off the train. I spent my Saturday nights in New York because those gleaming, dazzling parties of his were with me so vividly that I could still hear the music and the laughter, faint and incessant, from his garden, and the cars going up and down his drive. One night I did hear a material car there, and saw its lights stop at his front steps. But I didn’t investigate. Probably it was some final guest who had been away at the ends of the earth and didn’t know that the party was over. On the last night, with my trunk packed and my car sold to the grocer, I went over and looked at that huge incoherent failure of a house once more. On the white steps an obscene word, scrawled by some boy with a piece of brick, stood out clearly in the moonlight, and I erased it, drawing my shoe raspingly along the stone. Then I wandered down to the beach and sprawled out on the sand. Most of the big shore places were closed now and there were hardly any lights except the shadowy, moving glow of a ferryboat across the Sound. And as the moon rose higher the inessential houses began to melt away until gradually I became aware of the old island here that flowered once for Dutch sailors’ eyes—a fresh, green breast of the new world. Its vanished trees, the trees that had made way for Gatsby’s house, had once pandered in whispers to the last and greatest of all human dreams; for a transitory enchanted moment man must have held his breath in the presence of this continent, compelled into an aesthetic contemplation he neither understood nor desired, face to face for the last time in history with something commensurate to his capacity for wonder. And as I sat there brooding on the old, unknown world, I thought of Gatsby’s wonder when he first picked out the green light at the end of Daisy’s dock. He had come a long way to this blue lawn, and his dream must have seemed so close that he could hardly fail to grasp it. He did not know that it was already behind him, somewhere back in that vast obscurity beyond the city, where the dark fields of the republic rolled on under the night. Gatsby believed in the green light, the orgiastic future that year by year recedes before us. It eluded us then, but that’s no matter—tomorrow we will run faster, stretch out our arms further … And one fine morning— So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past.
S-THE-044.summary: Nick, reflecting on his disillusionment with the East, breaks off his relationship with Jordan, who reveals her engagement and accuses him of carelessness. Later, he confronts Tom Buchanan on Fifth Avenue, where Tom defiantly admits his role in Gatsby's death but frames it as deserved, revealing his utter lack of remorse and profound self-pity.

nodes.content: His father's advice to reserve judgment and his own attempt to live by it, his family background and decision to move East for the bond business.
His father's advice to reserve judgment, which he tries to uphold but admits has limits.
Comes from a prominent, well-to-do Middle Western family, but feels the wealth of the East is on another level.
Graduated from New Haven in 1915, fought in the Great War, and moved East in 1922 to learn the bond business.
Notices the superficial differences and underlying tensions between East Egg and West Egg, and the peculiar nature of the wealthy.
Perceives Tom Buchanan as physically imposing and arrogant, Daisy as charming but superficial, and Jordan Baker as self-sufficient and discontented.
Holds an unaffected scorn for 'everything for which Gatsby stood' but acknowledges Gatsby's 'extraordinary gift for hope' and 'romantic readiness'.
Home of the Carraway family for three generations, known for their wholesale hardware business.
Represents traditional values and a certain provincialism when compared to the East.
Contains the 'two unusual formations of land' known as West Egg and East Egg.
Referred to as the 'great wet barnyard of Long Island Sound'.
The more fashionable and affluent of the two eggs, featuring 'white palaces' along the water.
Home to Tom and Daisy Buchanan's elaborate Georgian Colonial mansion.
His house location in West Egg, next to Gatsby's mansion, and his awareness of the social distinctions between East and West Egg.
One of two 'enormous eggs' on a riotous island east of New York, separated from East Egg by a courtesy bay.
Home to Jay Gatsby's mansion, a 'factual imitation of some Hôtel de Ville in Normandy'.
His observation of Tom's aggressive arrogance, Daisy's captivating but superficial nature, Jordan's self-sufficiency, and the strained dynamics of the Buchanan household.
The residence of Tom and Daisy Buchanan, signifying their established wealth and social standing.
Tom Buchanan has a mistress in New York.
Daisy Buchanan is deeply unhappy in her marriage and cynical about life.
Miss Baker is Jordan Baker, a professional golfer with a questionable past.
Mr. Gatsby is his mysterious neighbor who lives in West Egg.
Gatsby displays a curious longing gesture towards a green light across the bay.
He learns about Tom's affair with a woman in New York from Miss Baker.
He feels uncomfortable and alienated by the domestic tension in the Buchanan household.
He observes Gatsby for the first time, a mysterious figure reaching out towards a green light across the bay.
He is taken by Tom to meet his mistress, Myrtle Wilson, in the Valley of Ashes.
He observes the stark class differences between the Buchanans' world and the Valley of Ashes/Myrtle's apartment.
He becomes drunk for only the second time in his life at Myrtle's apartment, blurring his memory of events.
The setting for the initial strained dinner party, where domestic discord is thinly veiled.
Home to Tom and Daisy Buchanan, symbolizing their wealth and social standing.
Nick Carraway's residence and home to the mysterious Mr. Gatsby.
Owned by George B. Wilson, it's where Tom Buchanan meets his mistress, Myrtle.
The place where Tom conducts his affair with Myrtle Wilson.
He meets George B. Wilson, a struggling garage owner and Myrtle's husband.
He meets Myrtle Wilson, Tom's mistress.
Home to George and Myrtle Wilson's garage and apartment.
A stark contrast to the opulence of East and West Egg, representing the working class and the consequences of careless wealth.
He gets drunk at Myrtle's party, blurring his memory of events.
He meets Catherine, Myrtle's sister, and Mr. McKee, a neighbor.
The setting for the party hosted by Tom and Myrtle, where social boundaries blur and inhibitions fade.
Nick's observation of the moral decay and superficiality of the social scene, feeling 'within and without, simultaneously enchanted and repelled.'
Observes the affected behavior of Mrs. Wilson and the dysfunctional relationships at the apartment party.
Hears scandalous and conflicting rumors about Jay Gatsby's past.
Feels ill at ease and awkward at Gatsby's party, despite being invited.
A secret rendezvous for Tom Buchanan and Myrtle Wilson.
Paneled with carved English oak, possibly imported from overseas.
Nick's receipt of a personalized invitation to Gatsby's party, distinguishing him from the majority of guests, and his growing fascination with Gatsby's enigmatic persona.
The site of Gatsby's famous, uncurated parties.
Rumored source of Gatsby's mysterious wealth.
Owl Eyes was brought by Mrs. Claud Roosevelt and has been drunk for a week.
He reflects on his own honesty as a 'cardinal virtue' and acknowledges his internal 'rules' that temper his desires.
He initially assumes Gatsby would be a 'florid and corpulent person in his middle years,' showing his preconceived notions of wealth.
He feels 'tender curiosity' for Jordan Baker and a momentary delusion of love.
He is still entangled with a girl 'back home' from whom he needs to tactfully break off.
The books are surprisingly real, not cardboard props.
Gatsby is the host of the party.
Gatsby served in the Sixteenth Infantry during the war.
Gatsby owns a hydroplane and invites Nick to fly in it.
Jordan suspects Gatsby lied about attending Oxford.
Gatsby is frequently called on the phone, notably from 'Chicago'.
Gatsby is seen observing his party guests but not participating in their revelry or drinking.
Jordan is summoned to speak privately with Gatsby.
Site of Gatsby's extravagant parties.
Jordan has learned something 'amazing' about Gatsby but won't share it.
Jordan's aunt is Mrs. Sigourney Howard.
Owl Eyes was a passenger in the crashed car, not the driver.
He works at Probity Trust as a bond-salesman.
He had a short affair with a girl in Jersey City that ended due to her brother's disapproval.
He studies investments and securities at the Yale Club.
He perceives Jordan Baker as 'incurably dishonest' and recalls a past golf scandal where she moved her ball.
He has an unresolved past relationship 'back home' that he needs to end.
He considers himself one of the few honest people he knows.
A place of anonymity and opportunity for Nick.
An extensive list of Gatsby's party guests, their origins, and some of their peculiar or scandalous backstories.
Observes the transient and superficial nature of Gatsby's party guests.
Doubts Gatsby's fabricated stories about his past, particularly his Oxford education and the origin of his wealth.
Is staggered by the revelation that Meyer Wolfshiem fixed the 1919 World Series, reflecting on the corruption of institutions.
Is annoyed that Gatsby's "big request" must be conveyed through Jordan Baker rather than directly.
Learns about the past romantic connection between Gatsby and Daisy from Jordan Baker.
Site of Gatsby's lavish parties that attract a diverse but mostly unknown crowd.
Reputation for attracting notorious figures and bizarre incidents.
Home to the Wilsons' garage and Myrtle Wilson.
Symbolizes a gateway to new possibilities and the mysterious allure of New York City.
A place where anything can happen, embodying the 'wild promise' of the world.
The site of Rosy Rosenthal's murder, a notorious event in Meyer Wolfshiem's past.
Daisy Fay was the most popular girl here in 1917.
Gatsby's fabricated backstory: son of wealthy Midwest parents, educated at Oxford, war hero (Major), collected jewels, traveled Europe, awarded medals by Allied governments.
Gatsby's connection to a police commissioner, allowing him to avoid tickets.
Gatsby has a 'big request' that Jordan Baker will relay.
Features Port Roosevelt, indicating maritime industry.
Passes through the 'valley of ashes,' a desolate industrial wasteland.
Meyer Wolfshiem is Gatsby's business associate.
Meyer Wolfshiem fixed the 1919 World Series.
Gatsby is embarrassed and disappears upon meeting Tom Buchanan.
A regular meeting place for Gatsby and Wolfshiem, frequented by those involved in less-than-legal activities.
Details of Daisy Fay's past popularity and social status in Louisville.
That Daisy Fay and Jay Gatsby had a romantic relationship in 1917.
Rumors that Daisy almost ran away to New York to see Gatsby before he went overseas.
Becomes instrumental in arranging the meeting between Gatsby and Daisy.
Feels a growing physical attraction to Jordan Baker.
Recounts Daisy's past and Gatsby's elaborate plan from his perspective.
Observes Gatsby's extreme nervousness and attempts to reassure him during the reunion.
Daisy had a 'perfect reputation' in Chicago despite a 'fast crowd.'
Refuses Gatsby's offer of a 'confidential sort of thing' business, implying it's illicit.
Gatsby bought it specifically to be across the bay from Daisy.
A brewer originally built it, agreeing to pay taxes for neighbors who thatched their roofs.
Nick observes Gatsby's intense emotional reaction to Daisy and the shift in his perception of the green light.
Contains Marie Antoinette music-rooms, Restoration Salons, period bedrooms, dressing-rooms, poolrooms, and bathrooms with sunken baths.
Home to a 'Merton College Library' and an Adam's study.
Features a large postern gate and marble steps.
Nick learns the full story of Gatsby's past, including his original name, his relationship with Dan Cody, and the source of his ambition.
Nick perceives the class differences and social snobbery of the Sloanes and Tom Buchanan towards Gatsby.
Nick observes Daisy's flirtatious behavior at the party and the underlying tension between Gatsby and Tom.
Daisy's cynical nature and discomfort with the West Egg crowd.
He notes Daisy wasn't having a good time at Gatsby's party despite her initial genially.
He understands Gatsby's 'unutterable depression' over Daisy's reaction and his desperate desire to repeat the past, even witnessing Gatsby's memory of kissing Daisy five years ago.
He observes Gatsby's vulnerability and distress over Daisy's reaction to the party and his later, more controlled appearance at the Buchanans'.
He understands that Daisy's voice is 'full of money,' realizing it's the source of her charm and allure.
Once a hub of extravagant parties, now a more private, exclusive residence.
Gatsby's profound depression over Daisy's reaction and his desperate desire to revert to a past state.
His insight that Daisy's voice is 'full of money,' signifying its inherent charm and power.
He observes the profound difference between the 'sick' and the 'well' through Wilson's despair.
Observes the class and moral distinctions between the 'sick' and the 'well' in the context of Wilson's despair.
Judges Tom's hypocrisy and racism.
Reflects on turning thirty and the implications for his future and relationship with Jordan.
A place of toil and faded dreams, overseen by the symbolic eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg.
He learns the true nature of Gatsby's 'Oxford' education and the criminal source of his wealth.
A site where social veneers crack under emotional pressure.
He turns thirty, reflecting on the 'portentous, menacing road of a new decade' and his feelings of loneliness.
A symbolic journey back to the perceived comfort and disillusionment of the wealthy suburbs.
He witnesses Tom's manipulation and is privy to his lie about the yellow car.
He feels sickened by the events and the people involved, including Jordan, and wants to be alone.
He is one of two people who hear Tom's lie about the yellow car.
He becomes Gatsby's confidante, learning the truth about Daisy driving and Gatsby's past with her.
He observes Daisy and Tom's 'conspiring' intimacy, leading him to doubt their happiness and Gatsby's hopes.
Site of Myrtle Wilson's fatal hit-and-run accident.
The place where Myrtle Wilson's body is brought after the accident.
Wilson's office is located within the garage, a small, enclosed space.
He is so disgusted with the events and people that he wants to be alone and avoid the Buchanans.
He observes Tom and Daisy in the kitchen, appearing to be 'conspiring' and not truly unhappy, despite the tragic events.
The residence of Tom and Daisy Buchanan, a symbol of their wealth and social standing.
Daisy's room is on the ground floor, marked by a pink glow.
The setting for a tense, intimate conversation between Tom and Daisy after the accident.
He gains a deeper understanding of Gatsby's complex motivations and the true depth of his romantic idealism and past deceptions.
Site of Gatsby's deep emotional connection to Daisy, built in anticipation of her return.
The scene of Gatsby's confidences to Nick about his past and love for Daisy.
His conflicted moral judgment of Gatsby – disapproving of his life but convinced of his inherent worth compared to others.
Acknowledges his disapproval of Gatsby from beginning to end, yet values him above 'the whole damn bunch'.
Finds himself increasingly isolated in his care for Gatsby after his death, responsible for his affairs as no one else is interested.
Experiences profound disillusionment as he witnesses the indifference of others to Gatsby's death and the falsification of events.
Attempts to contact Meyer Wolfshiem and Daisy to arrange Gatsby's funeral, only to be met with disinterest and evasion.
The site of Gatsby's ultimate fate and the culmination of his dream.
The city where Daisy Fay lived and where Gatsby sought her after the war.
A symbolic location representing moral decay and the discarded dreams of the American working class.
His increasing indifference towards his relationship with Jordan, prioritizing Gatsby and the unfolding tragedy.
The site of Myrtle Wilson's death and George's subsequent vigil.
His immediate alarm and the realization of the profound tragedy upon discovering Gatsby's body.
His profound disillusionment and sense of abandonment as he attempts to arrange Gatsby's funeral, facing indifference from all sides.

Nick is deeply disillusioned by the lack of attendance at Gatsby's funeral, the self-serving nature of Gatsby's associates, and the moral emptiness of the Buchanan's. He also reflects on his own complicity and growth.
Nick concludes that the 'West' represents a moral foundation, while the 'East' fosters carelessness and corruption, leading him to decide to return home.
Nick ends his relationship with Jordan Baker, acknowledging their incompatibility and her perceived dishonesty.
Nick judges Tom and Daisy Buchanan as 'careless people' who destroy things and retreat into their wealth.
Site of Gatsby's lavish parties, now a symbol of his isolation and the superficiality of his social connections.
The setting for Gatsby's eventual funeral, revealing the stark reality of his true friendships.
The place where Wolfshiem conducted his business, revealing the criminal underworld Gatsby was connected to.
The burial site for Gatsby, where the lack of mourners underscores his social isolation despite his wealth.
Represents the 'new money' and the striving, yet ultimately flawed, American Dream.
Symbolizes the moral decay and superficiality that Nick associates with Eastern life.
Nick's origin and moral compass, contrasting with the moral ambiguity of the East.
Represents a sense of identity and belonging that the East lacks for Nick.
Nick learns the extent of Gatsby's loneliness and the superficiality of his 'friends'.
Nick observes the lack of mourners and Daisy's complete absence, cementing his view of the East's superficiality.
Nick recognizes Tom and Daisy as 'careless people' who smash things and retreat into their money.
His advice to Nick about judgment and his role in the family business.
Advised Nick to reserve judgment, understanding that not everyone has had the same advantages.
Carries on the wholesale hardware business started by his great-uncle.
Agreed to finance Nick for a year in the East.
That he is the man the book is named after, exempt from Nick's scorn, possessing an 'extraordinary gift for hope' and 'romantic readiness'.
He owns a colossal, imitation French mansion in West Egg, indicative of immense wealth.
Owns a colossal mansion in West Egg, a factual imitation of a Hôtel de Ville in Normandy.
Possesses an 'extraordinary gift for hope' and 'romantic readiness', making him exempt from Nick's general scorn for the privileged.
Known to Miss Baker, who assumes Nick would know him because they live in West Egg.
He is observed by Nick making a curious gesture, stretching his arms towards a green light across the bay, appearing to tremble.
He lives in a mansion next door to Nick in West Egg.
The widespread and fantastical nature of rumors circulating about Gatsby (Kaiser Wilhelm's nephew, German spy, murderer), highlighting his mysterious reputation.
Becomes the subject of various rumors regarding the source of his wealth and his past activities (Kaiser Wilhelm's nephew, German spy, murderer).
His lavish parties attract a wide and uninvited crowd, demonstrating his social impact and mysterious persona.
Sends Nick a formal invitation, suggesting a degree of personal interest or formality not extended to others.
Remains unseen and difficult to find even at his own party, adding to his mystique.
He is revealed to be the host of the party, much to Nick's surprise.
Jordan mentions he claimed to be an 'Oxford man,' though she doesn't believe it, adding to his mysterious background.
He stands apart from his guests, not drinking, maintaining a more 'correct' demeanor amidst the revelry.
He is constantly receiving calls, including from 'Chicago' and 'Philadelphia,' hinting at business dealings.
Guests are circulating rumors that he is a bootlegger and possibly a murderer.
The details of his fabricated past, including war heroics, Oxford education, and European travels.
That he seeks to convey a 'big request' to Nick through Jordan Baker.
His ability to evade legal consequences (speeding ticket) due to connections.
Attempts to convince Nick of his fabricated past as a wealthy, Oxford-educated heir and war hero to legitimize himself before making a request.
Carries a war medal and an Oxford photo as props to support his false narrative, demonstrating resourcefulness in his deception.
Has a "big request" that he needs Jordan Baker to relay to Nick, the nature of which is initially unclear but later revealed to be related to Daisy.
Is deeply connected to Meyer Wolfshiem, a known gambler and World Series fixer, suggesting illicit sources of his wealth and influence.
Is revealed to have had a romantic relationship with Daisy Fay in 1917, before the war.
His association with Meyer Wolfshiem, a known criminal.
His discomfort when introduced to Tom Buchanan, leading him to disappear.
His past romantic involvement with Daisy Fay in 1917.
Bought his house specifically to be near Daisy, having waited five years to see her again.
Afraid to ask Daisy directly and initially attempts to abandon the idea of meeting her when Tom is mentioned.
Implies a 'side business' and a 'confidential sort of thing' to Nick, suggesting illicit activities, and later gives a contradictory explanation of his inherited wealth.
Arranges for Nick's lawn to be cut, sends flowers, and meticulously prepares for Daisy's arrival.
Implies a 'side business' and a 'confidential sort of thing' to Nick, suggesting illicit activities.
Displays profound anxiety, paleness, sleeplessness, and awkwardness before and during the initial reunion with Daisy.
Transforms from distraught to glowing with happiness after his reunion with Daisy.
Gives a contradictory explanation of his inherited wealth, mentioning the 'drug business' and 'oil business'.
Beaming with pride, he questions Nick about his impression of his house and boasts about his wealth.
Gatsby's long-held dream of Daisy's presence in his house is realized, leading to an emotional and almost disoriented state.
Gatsby re-evaluates his possessions through Daisy's eyes, and the symbolic significance of the green light diminishes now that she is physically present.
Gatsby's true identity as James Gatz and his formative years with Dan Cody are described, revealing the origins of his wealth and persona.
Gatsby attempts to impress Tom and the Sloanes, and later tries to make Tom jealous by flaunting his connection to Daisy and his party guests.
Gatsby is visibly uneasy and somewhat inept in social interactions with the Buchanans and Sloanes, compared to his usual controlled demeanor.
His belief that he can 'repeat the past' and 'fix everything just the way it was before' with Daisy.
His deep unhappiness over Daisy's negative reaction to his party.
He dismissed his entire staff and replaced them with 'Wolfsheim's people' to avoid gossip, specifically to facilitate Daisy's visits.
He believes he can 'fix everything just the way it was before' with Daisy, clinging to a five-year-old memory of their kiss.
He is deeply depressed by Daisy's dislike of his party, seeing it as a failure to connect with his vision of their past.
He dismisses his entire staff to replace them with Wolfsheim's associates to prevent gossip, specifically to facilitate Daisy's visits.
He endures Tom's hostility and Daisy's open declarations of love, suggesting a deep commitment to her.
He seems surprised by Pammy's existence, suggesting he hadn't fully integrated her into his idealized vision of Daisy.
His surprise at Pammy's existence, suggesting a disconnect between his idealized Daisy and her reality as a mother.
Reluctantly clarifies his Oxford attendance, revealing it was a post-WWI officer program, not traditional enrollment.
Declares his love for Daisy and asserts she never loved Tom, attempting to rewrite their shared history.
Is accused by Tom of being a bootlegger through the 'drugstore' business, revealing a key source of his wealth.
Recognizes the 'dead dream' of his relationship with Daisy as she falters in her commitment.
He attended Oxford for five months as part of a post-WWI officer program.
He believes Daisy never loved Tom and only married him out of convenience.
He confesses that Daisy was driving the car that killed Myrtle and that he will take the blame, revealing his ultimate loyalty and protective instinct.
He confesses to Nick that Daisy was driving the car that killed Myrtle Wilson and that he intends to take the blame.
He plans to wait outside the Buchanan house all night to ensure Tom doesn't bother Daisy.
He reveals the detailed history of his relationship with Daisy, including his initial deception about his wealth and status.
He maintains an idealistic view of Daisy's potential love for him, despite evidence to the contrary.
He reveals he actively deceived Daisy about his social standing and wealth when they first met, taking her under 'false pretenses' for love.
He describes Daisy as the first 'nice' girl he knew, captivated by her world of wealth and glamour, leading to an almost religious devotion.

His intense, personal conception of his affair with Daisy, rooted in his desperate post-war journey to Louisville, where he sought traces of her, believing he'd lost 'the freshest and the best' part of it forever.
Recalculates the intensity of his past affair with Daisy, recalling his miserable but irresistible journey to Louisville, searching for her after her wedding.
Expresses continued hope that Daisy will call him, even after the tragic events.
Is suggested to have felt a sense of loss and disillusionment, realizing the high price for living too long with a single dream, just before his death.
His ultimate demise by murder, fulfilling a tragic destiny tied to his dream.
His physical description, arrogant demeanor, past athletic fame, and his racist ideology about 'Nordics' and 'coloured empires'.
Enormously wealthy, from old money, and married to Nick's cousin Daisy.
Was a national figure in football at New Haven, reaching an 'acute limited excellence' at twenty-one.
Arrogant, physically imposing, and possessing a 'cruel body' and a 'fractious' voice.
Believes in 'Nordic' racial superiority and expresses fear of other races controlling things, referencing 'The Rise of the Coloured Empires'.
He is having an extramarital affair.
He is having an affair with a woman in New York, which causes tension in his marriage.
He exercises social dominance, forcing Nick to meet his mistress and displaying open contempt for George Wilson.
He maintains the facade that his mistress visits her sister in New York, deceiving her husband.
He discusses the 'Nordic race' with Daisy and advises Nick not to believe 'everything you hear,' implying a belief in racial superiority.
Tom's violent temper and possessiveness over Daisy's name, leading him to physically assault Myrtle.
Asserts his control over the party guests and Myrtle, ordering them around.
Mocks George Wilson and suggests Myrtle give McKee a letter of introduction to him for a photography project, revealing his disdain.
Violently breaks Myrtle Wilson's nose for mentioning Daisy's name.
The identity of Jay Gatsby, whom he briefly meets.
Is eager to know where Nick has been and questions his lunch with Gatsby, revealing his possessive nature and slight suspicion.
Meets Gatsby for the first time without realizing Gatsby's past romantic history with his wife, Daisy.
Cheated on Daisy shortly after their marriage, evidenced by a car accident with a chambermaid.
Tom actively dismisses Gatsby's attempts at friendliness and later shows disdain for Gatsby's party and guests.
Tom displays possessiveness over Daisy and suspicion regarding her time with Gatsby.
Tom judges Gatsby's guests and lifestyle, referring to them as 'crazy fish' and expressing a desire to be in 'oblivion' among them.
His immediate suspicion that Gatsby is a 'big bootlegger'.
He suspects Gatsby is a 'big bootlegger' and intends to find out more about his business.
He recognizes Daisy's 'I love you' for Gatsby, leading to visible tension, trembling, and a desire to control the situation by forcing everyone to go to town.
He reveals he's been 'making a small investigation' into Gatsby's background, dismissing Jordan's 'Oxford man' suggestion incredulously.
His immediate and clear understanding that Daisy's 'you look so cool' to Gatsby is a declaration of love.
His barely suppressed rage and trembling hands after Daisy's public affection for Gatsby.
His revelation that he has made a 'small investigation' into Gatsby's past, and his incredulity at the 'Oxford man' claim.
Experiences panic as his control over Daisy and Myrtle slips, driving recklessly.
Accuses Gatsby of being a 'swindler' and a 'bootlegger', revealing his knowledge of Gatsby's illegal activities.
Asserts his place as Daisy's husband despite her confession of love for Gatsby.
He knows Gatsby is a bootlegger involved in selling grain alcohol through 'drugstores' with Meyer Wolfshiem.
He immediately takes charge of the accident scene, attempting to control information and protect himself, actively lying about the yellow car not being his.
Initially insensitive about the accident, he quickly takes charge to cover up his link to the yellow car and later cries over Myrtle's death.
He explicitly lies to the policeman and others about the yellow car not being his and that he hasn't seen it all afternoon, redirecting suspicion.
He forcefully takes Wilson into his office and ensures he is watched, preventing him from speaking further.
He expresses remorse ('God damned coward!') but also relief that the car didn't stop.
Leaves town with Daisy immediately after Gatsby's death, without leaving a forwarding address.
Tom admits he told Wilson that Gatsby owned the yellow car.
Tom believes Gatsby 'had it coming to him' and justifies his actions by claiming Gatsby ran over Myrtle and threw 'dust' in Daisy's eyes.
Tom confesses to telling Wilson that Gatsby owned the car that killed Myrtle, but justifies his actions by claiming Gatsby 'had it coming to him' and was a 'tough one'.
Tom expresses self-pity, recounting his tears over seeing dog biscuits in his old flat, believing he suffered greatly.
Tom demonstrates a profound lack of empathy and self-awareness, viewing his destructive actions as justified and Gatsby's death as deserved.
Her captivating voice, hints of sadness, her daughter, and her apparent unhappiness with Tom, evidenced by her accusation about her finger and her reaction to Tom's racist remarks.
Nick Carraway's second cousin once removed, married to Tom Buchanan.
Lives in a white palace in fashionable East Egg, part of the 'old money' elite.
Possesses a captivating 'singing compulsion' in her voice and a seemingly effortless charm, but with hints of underlying sadness and superficiality.
Has a three-year-old daughter.
Accuses Tom of hurting her finger, calling him a 'brute', and shows some awareness of his racist views, though she doesn't actively challenge them.
She knows about Tom's affair and is visibly distressed by it.
She is deeply unhappy in her marriage, revealed by her emotional distress and cynical pronouncement about hoping her daughter would be a 'beautiful little fool'.
She is aware of Tom's affair and is visibly distressed by it, using 'tense gaiety' to mask her feelings.
She shares a past 'white girlhood' with Jordan Baker in Louisville.
Her past romantic involvement with Jay Gatsby.
Her attempt to leave Louisville to say goodbye to Gatsby, thwarted by her family.
Is revealed to have been romantically involved with Jay Gatsby in 1917.
Was the most popular young girl in Louisville in 1917, highly sought after by officers.
Attempted to run away to New York to say goodbye to Gatsby before he went overseas, indicating strong feelings for him that were thwarted by her family.
On her wedding day, she was drunk and tried to call off the wedding after receiving a letter, implied to be from Gatsby.
Initially appeared madly in love with Tom, expressing unease when he left her sight.
On her wedding day to Tom Buchanan, she was drunk and tried to call off the wedding after receiving a letter, implied to be from Gatsby.
Initially unaware of Gatsby's motivation for buying his mansion or his desire to meet her, though she recognized his name.
Her tears and subsequent radiant happiness upon seeing Gatsby confirm deep, lingering feelings.
Displays nervousness, embarrassment, and then overwhelming joy during her reunion with Gatsby.
Asks Gatsby if he inherited his money, indicating an awareness of social standing and wealth origin.
Daisy experiences Gatsby's wealth and devotion firsthand, overwhelmed by his possessions and the intensity of his feelings.
Daisy notices a 'harshness' in the atmosphere at Gatsby's party with Tom present, sensing the underlying tension and class differences.
Daisy flirts openly with Nick and shows excessive admiration for Gatsby's possessions, possibly to provoke Tom or simply indulging in the moment.
Daisy is excited by the celebrities at Gatsby's party, contrasting with Tom's disdain for them.
Her disgust for the 'raw vigour' of West Egg and its inhabitants.
She finds West Egg's 'raw vigour' and the social climbing of its inhabitants appalling, despite initially finding it interesting. She does not enjoy Gatsby's party.
She kisses Gatsby on the mouth and murmurs 'You know I love you' in front of Nick and Jordan, and later tells him he looks 'cool' in front of Tom, which Tom interprets as a declaration of love.

She arranges to have Gatsby's servants replaced and calls Nick herself to confirm his attendance for lunch at her house, implying a desire to facilitate her affair with Gatsby. She pushes for the trip to town after revealing her affection for Gatsby.
She presents her daughter, Pammy, to Gatsby and Nick, emphasizing Pammy's resemblance to herself.
Her open, physical declaration of love for Gatsby in front of Nick and Jordan.
Her deliberate choice to ride with Gatsby, defying Tom's implicit instruction.
She admits to loving Gatsby now, but cannot truthfully say she never loved Tom.
Struggles to fully disavow her past love for Tom, torn between her present desire for Gatsby and her history.
Becomes increasingly distraught and overwhelmed by the confrontation between Tom and Gatsby.
She was driving the 'death car' and lost her nerve after hitting Myrtle.
She was driving the car that killed Myrtle Wilson, lost her nerve, and drove on.
She is described as nervous and later locks herself in her room, using a signal if Tom tries to be 'brutal'.
Her past reveals a need for security and stability, leading to her marriage to Tom despite her love for Gatsby.
She is revealed to have married Tom due to her need for security and a decision about her life, influenced by Gatsby's absence and the pressures of her social world.
Her callous abandonment of Gatsby, fleeing with Tom without a trace after his death.
Leaves town with Tom immediately after Gatsby's death, without leaving a forwarding address, effectively abandoning Gatsby.
Daisy sends no message or flower to Gatsby's funeral, highlighting her complete emotional detachment and self-preservation.
Nick concludes that Daisy, like Tom, is a 'careless person' who smashes things and retreats into her wealth.
Her profession as a golfer, her self-sufficient and somewhat bored demeanor, and her knowledge of Gatsby.
A friend of Daisy Buchanan who is visiting the Buchanans.
A professional golfer, currently 'in training'.
Self-sufficient, somewhat aloof, and observant, with a 'wan, charming, discontented face'.
Seems to know Gatsby, or at least knows of his presence in West Egg.
She is aware of Tom's affair.
She knows about Tom's affair and attempts to listen in on a phone call related to it.
She is Jordan Baker, a professional golfer, recognized by Nick from rotogravure pictures.
Nick recalls hearing an 'unpleasant story' about her, though he cannot remember the details.
Jordan's social ease and her cynical observations about the party, as well as her participation in the gossip about Gatsby.
Navigates Gatsby's party with ease, immediately recognizing Nick and engaging in social banter.
Offers sharp observations about other party guests and their behavior.
Shows contemptuous interest in the party-goers and later finds a 'polite' party too boring.
Leans in confidentially to share rumors about Gatsby, indicating her engagement with the social undercurrents.
Nick learns she is incurably dishonest, having lied about a golf scandal and a borrowed car incident.
Nick experiences a moment of perceived love for her, despite her flaws.
She is a golf champion and well-known in social circles.
She shares an 'amazing thing' with Nick about Gatsby but refuses to elaborate, tantalizing him.
She lied about leaving a borrowed car in the rain.
She was involved in a golf scandal where she was accused of moving her ball.
She believes others will avoid her reckless driving, stating 'It takes two to make an accident'.
Is entrusted by Gatsby to convey a "big request" to Nick, positioning her as a go-between.
Recounts her memory of Daisy and Gatsby's past romantic relationship in 1917.
Was aware of Daisy's popularity and social standing in Louisville in 1917, and the rumors surrounding her engagement.
Was Daisy's bridesmaid and witnessed her attempt to call off the wedding.
Relates the history of Daisy's marriage and Gatsby's motivations to Nick.
Suggests the tea meeting between Daisy and Gatsby and explains Gatsby's fear.
Comments on Daisy's social reputation and the advantages of not drinking in a hard-drinking crowd.

She remains relatively detached from Daisy's emotional state, offering practical advice like 'Life starts all over again when it gets crisp in the fall.'
Observes the escalating tension between Tom and Gatsby, often with a sardonic or detached comment.
Deflects Tom's racist tirade by pointing out their shared whiteness.
Judges Biloxi's behavior and implicitly Tom's.
She accompanies Nick and Tom to the accident scene and subsequently to the Buchanan house, but is later implicitly rejected by Nick.
Calls Nick to inform him she has left Daisy's house and expresses annoyance at Nick's behavior the previous night, leading to a strained, unresolved conversation.
Jordan reveals she is engaged to another man.
Jordan believes Nick is a 'bad driver' and not as honest as she thought.
Jordan reveals she is engaged to another man and confronts Nick for 'throwing her over' on the phone.
Jordan accuses Nick of being a bad driver, metaphorically implying his carelessness in relationships, and expresses disappointment in his perceived dishonesty.
She is Tom's mistress and treats her husband with contempt.
She desires social climbing and a more sophisticated life.
She is Tom Buchanan's mistress, living in the Valley of Ashes.
She aspires to a more sophisticated lifestyle, as evidenced by her purchases and desire for an apartment dog.
She treats her husband, George Wilson, with contempt, walking 'through' him and speaking to him dismissively.

She quickly establishes familiarity with Nick, calling him by his first name.
Myrtle's contempt for her husband, George Wilson, whom she married under the mistaken belief that he was a gentleman, and her recollection of her initial attraction to Tom Buchanan.
Transforms her personality to one of 'impressive hauteur' in her new dress, affecting grand gestures and speech.
Reveals her deep unhappiness with her husband George Wilson, whom she married believing he was a gentleman.

Is seen by Nick in the Valley of Ashes.
She mistakenly believes Jordan Baker is Tom's wife.
Misinterprets Jordan Baker as Tom's wife, displaying intense jealous terror.

Her past actions, particularly the dog leash and her bruised face, become evidence for George's suspicions of her infidelity.

He is unaware of his wife's affair with Tom, believing she visits her sister in New York.
He is easily manipulated and dismissed by Tom, who uses him for his own purposes.
Myrtle has a life apart from him; he suspects infidelity.
He wants to move West with Myrtle to escape his current situation.

Expresses a desperate desire to leave the Valley of Ashes with Myrtle, planning to go West.

He has locked Myrtle in her room as a response to her infidelity.
He is in profound grief and knows the car was yellow, making him a potential threat to Tom's cover-up.
He is in a state of extreme grief and shock over his wife's death, repeatedly crying out and being unable to hear or see.
He knows the car that hit his wife was yellow, even amidst his grief.
He is easily manipulated and silenced by Tom in his distraught state.
His deluded conviction, intensified by grief, that the owner of the yellow car is Myrtle's murderer and lover, and that divine retribution is at hand.
Sinks into profound grief and develops a deluded conviction that the driver of the yellow car, whom he believes was Myrtle's lover, murdered her.
Becomes determined to find and avenge Myrtle's death, believing 'God sees everything' through the eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg.
Murders Gatsby and then takes his own life.
His act of vengeful murder-suicide, the culmination of his grief and delusion.
She is Myrtle's sister and is present at the party.

Catherine's knowledge that Tom and Myrtle despise their spouses and her belief that Daisy's Catholicism prevents their divorce (which Nick realizes is a lie).

He lives below Myrtle and attends the party.

Chester McKee's photographic ambition and his passive, often overlooked, presence at the party.
Seeks professional opportunities on Long Island, hoping for an 'entry' to photograph wealthy individuals.
Remains largely passive and overlooked during the party, even falling asleep.

Expresses pride in her husband's photographic obsession with her.
Compliments Myrtle Wilson's dress, possibly with a hint of aspiration.

Lucille's experience of receiving a new evening gown from Gatsby after damaging one at a previous party, illustrating Gatsby's detached generosity.

Ella Kaye acquired Dan Cody's fortune, preventing Gatsby from receiving his inheritance through legal means.

Nick's origin and moral compass, contrasting with the moral ambiguity of the East.
Represents a sense of identity and belonging that the East lacks for Nick.

Represents the 'new money' and the striving, yet ultimately flawed, American Dream.
Symbolizes the moral decay and superficiality that Nick associates with Eastern life.

Home to Tom and Daisy Buchanan, symbolizing their wealth and social standing.
The residence of Tom and Daisy Buchanan, a symbol of their wealth and social standing.
Daisy's room is on the ground floor, marked by a pink glow.
The setting for a tense, intimate conversation between Tom and Daisy after the accident.
Home to George and Myrtle Wilson's garage and apartment.
A stark contrast to the opulence of East and West Egg, representing the working class and the consequences of careless wealth.
Home to the Wilsons' garage and Myrtle Wilson.
Site of Myrtle Wilson's fatal hit-and-run accident.
A symbolic location representing moral decay and the discarded dreams of the American working class.
The setting for the party hosted by Tom and Myrtle, where social boundaries blur and inhibitions fade.
A secret rendezvous for Tom Buchanan and Myrtle Wilson.
The site of Gatsby's famous, uncurated parties.
Rumored source of Gatsby's mysterious wealth.
Site of Gatsby's extravagant parties.
Site of Gatsby's lavish parties that attract a diverse but mostly unknown crowd.
Reputation for attracting notorious figures and bizarre incidents.
Gatsby bought it specifically to be across the bay from Daisy.
A brewer originally built it, agreeing to pay taxes for neighbors who thatched their roofs.

Home to a 'Merton College Library' and an Adam's study.
Features a large postern gate and marble steps.
Once a hub of extravagant parties, now a more private, exclusive residence.
Site of Gatsby's deep emotional connection to Daisy, built in anticipation of her return.
The scene of Gatsby's confidences to Nick about his past and love for Daisy.
The site of Gatsby's ultimate fate and the culmination of his dream.
Site of Gatsby's lavish parties, now a symbol of his isolation and the superficiality of his social connections.
The setting for Gatsby's eventual funeral, revealing the stark reality of his true friendships.

The city where Daisy Fay lived and where Gatsby sought her after the war.
Daisy had a 'perfect reputation' in Chicago despite a 'fast crowd.'

The burial site for Gatsby, where the lack of mourners underscores his social isolation despite his wealth.

threads.description: The mystery surrounding Jay Gatsby's true identity, wealth, and past.
Daisy Buchanan's deep unhappiness and cynicism about her life and marriage.
Nick Carraway's integration into the wealthy social circles of West Egg and East Egg, and his evolving perspective on their values and lifestyles.
The social and class distinctions between old money (East Egg) and new money (West Egg).
The illicit affair between Tom Buchanan and Myrtle Wilson.
The nature of the American Dream and its corruption.

relationships.type: Confirmation of marriage based on practicality and security over love
Childhood friends, with a hint of shared social understanding.
Forced familiarity and observation of social performance.
Physical violence as a consequence of boundary violation

nodes.content: Gatsby's books are 'absolutely real,' which surprisingly fooled him.
He was the patron of Gatsby's library, discovered to be a reckless, non-driving party guest involved in a car accident.
Owl-Eyes is the only 'friend' from Gatsby's parties to attend the funeral, highlighting his genuine character.
Owl-Eyes is the only previous party guest who attends Gatsby's funeral, showing genuine respect.
Owl-Eyes recognizes the hypocrisy of Gatsby's former guests, noting how they flocked to his parties but deserted his funeral.
He served in the Sixteenth Infantry during the war, near where Nick served.
He was the passenger in the crashed car, unaware of the accident's severity.
Nick had a brief affair with her, which ended when her brother gave him 'mean looks'.
His criminal act of fixing the 1919 World Series.
His business association with Gatsby.
Is revealed to be the man who fixed the 1919 World Series, establishing his notorious criminal past.
Is Gatsby's business associate, indicating Gatsby's involvement in illicit activities.
Displays a sentimental attachment to the past and old friends, particularly recalling the murder of Rosy Rosenthal at the Metropole.
Notes Nick's presence and initially mistakes him for a potential "business gonnegtion," showing his astute and suspicious nature.
Gatsby replaces his household staff with 'brothers and sisters' whom Wolfsheim 'wanted to do something for', suggesting they are his associates.
His connection to Gatsby's new, shady staff, who are 'brothers and sisters' and used to run a small hotel.
His calculated refusal to involve himself in Gatsby's funeral, revealing the transactional nature of their relationship.
Refuses to attend Gatsby's funeral or get involved in the aftermath, citing 'important business' and claiming not to know Gatsby's family.
Wolfshiem 'made' Gatsby, giving him his start in business after the war.
Wolfshiem refuses to get 'mixed up' in Gatsby's death, revealing a code of self-preservation.
Wolfshiem recounts how he 'made' Gatsby, taking him from poverty and setting him up in business after the war.

Wolfshiem refuses to attend Gatsby's funeral, citing a personal rule to 'let everything alone' when a man is killed, implying a desire to avoid any connection to the criminal aspects of Gatsby's life.
Klipspringer is embarrassed when discovered doing exercises and later when forced to play piano for Gatsby and Daisy.
Klipspringer's status as a 'boarder' and his readiness to play piano on command highlight his reliance on Gatsby.
Klipspringer is a fair-weather friend, only concerned with his belongings, not Gatsby's death.
Klipspringer calls Nick, not to offer condolences or attend the funeral, but to ask about a forgotten pair of tennis shoes, demonstrating his self-serving nature.
He is collecting names and details about the accident, questioning witnesses about the car and events.
He turns away from suspicion of Tom after Nick and another witness confirm Tom's alibi.
The place where Tom conducts his affair with Myrtle Wilson.
A place of anonymity and opportunity for Nick.
A place where anything can happen, embodying the 'wild promise' of the world.
Paneled with carved English oak, possibly imported from overseas.
The books are surprisingly real, not cardboard props.
Features Port Roosevelt, indicating maritime industry.
Passes through the 'valley of ashes,' a desolate industrial wasteland.
A symbolic journey back to the perceived comfort and disillusionment of the wealthy suburbs.

A site where social veneers crack under emotional pressure.

threads.description: The strained and ambiguous relationship between Nick and Jordan Baker.
Nick Carraway's moral reckoning and his growing disillusionment with the American Dream and the people around him.
The social and moral decay masked by superficial glamour of the Jazz Age.

Gatsby's unstated desire or objective related to Nick and Jordan.
Gatsby's pursuit of Daisy's love and the idealized past he seeks to recapture

Cody's alcoholism influenced Gatsby's decision to abstain from drinking.
Cody left Gatsby a legacy that he never received due to legal maneuvering by Ella Kaye.
He was part of Gatsby's youth, contributing to his transformation and wealth.

Initially cordial, she invites Gatsby to supper, then subtly orchestrates his exclusion with Mr. Sloane.

He confirms there were two cars, one coming and one going, and that the one from New York hit Mrs. Wilson.
He confirms the car was a big, new yellow car going fast.
He states that the car was a yellow, big, new car, and that it passed him going very fast.
His accidental revelation of Gatsby's ongoing criminal activities, specifically bond fraud, through a phone call.

Gatsby's father believes his son was destined for greatness and was a good boy despite having run off.
